original_hypothesis.title: CloseExactOut Strict Equality Check Enables 1-Wei Griefing DoS on reducePos
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In coreCallback's CloseExactOut path, a strict equality check `_require(IERC20(swapInfo.tokenOut).balanceOf(address(this)) == swapInfo.amtOut, Errors.SLIPPAGE_CONTROL)` is used. An attacker can front-run any reducePos transaction by sending 1 wei of the borrow token (tokenOut) to the MarginTradingHook contract, causing the balance to be swapInfo.amtOut + 1, failing the equality check and reverting the user's transaction.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The strict equality (==) instead of (>=) creates a fragile check that can be trivially broken by anyone sending dust tokens to the contract. This is visible in the coreCallback function.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Line 328: '_require(IERC20(swapInfo.tokenOut).balanceOf(address(this)) == swapInfo.amtOut, Errors.SLIPPAGE_CONTROL)' uses == instead of >=. Any dust token deposit breaks this equality.

preconditions: User attempts reducePos with tokenOut == collToken (CloseExactOut swap type)
Attacker front-runs with 1 wei transfer of borrToken (swapInfo.tokenOut) to hook

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: balanceOf(address(this)) == swapInfo.amtOut fails when balance = amtOut + dust
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Front-run any CloseExactOut reducePos with 1 wei token transfer to hook address
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit for attacker, but griefing cost is ~1 wei + gas. Users cannot close their margin positions during adverse market conditions.

economic_questions: How many reduce position operations use CloseExactOut vs CloseExactIn?
Can this be sustained as a permanent DoS by repeatedly sending dust?
What is the user impact of being unable to reduce positions during market volatility?
validation_requirements: Send 1 wei of borrow token to hook before reducePos call
Verify revert at the equality check in coreCallback
Confirm CloseExactIn path is unaffected (no equality check)

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: 1 wei of borrow token per DoS attempt
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: ~0.005 ETH gas per front-run on Mantle
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect: prevents position closure during market crashes, potentially forcing liquidations
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Griefing attack; attacker profits by being the liquidator of stuck positions
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Persistent - must front-run each reducePos attempt

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During market volatility when users need to close positions
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Users unable to close positions may face liquidation

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Any user trying to reduce margin position via CloseExactOut
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Inability to close position → potential liquidation loss
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All margin positions attempting CloseExactOut reduction

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Verify == vs >= comparison in CloseExactOut path
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm balanceOf includes dust sent by attacker

validation_result.impact_summary: Any reducePos transaction using CloseExactOut swap type can be DoS'd by front-running with a 1-wei transfer of borrToken to the hook, preventing users from closing margin positions.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The coreCallback function in MarginTradingHook.sol uses a strict equality check `balanceOf(address(this)) == swapInfo.amtOut` for the CloseExactOut swap path. Since the MarginTradingHook contract address is publicly known and can receive arbitrary ERC20 token transfers, an attacker can send 1 wei of the borrow token (swapInfo.tokenOut) to the hook contract before the user's reducePos transaction executes. After the swap helper returns exactly `amtOut` tokens, the hook's balance becomes `amtOut + 1`, causing the strict equality check to fail and the entire transaction to revert. This is a classic griefing vulnerability where the attacker pays negligible cost (1 wei + gas) to deny service to any user attempting to reduce their margin position via CloseExactOut.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The vulnerable code is clearly visible in the coreCallback function. The strict equality check `==` should be `>=` to tolerate dust balances. The MoeSwapHelper's `swapTokensForExactTokens` sends exactly `amtOut` tokens to the hook (msg.sender), confirming that the expected balance is correct in normal operation but fragile against dust deposits. The `_refund` function in MoeSwapHelper only refunds `tokenIn`, not `tokenOut`, so it doesn't clear any pre-existing `tokenOut` balance.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The strict equality check (==) on balanceOf is fragile. Any dust token sent to the contract address before this check executes will cause it to fail, since balanceOf would return amtOut + dust instead of exactly amtOut. Should use >= instead of ==.

vulnerable_code_snippets.code_snippet:
SwapType swapType = _param.tokenOut == borrToken ? SwapType.CloseExactIn : SwapType.CloseExactOut;
SwapInfo memory swapInfo = SwapInfo(_param.initPosId, swapType, collToken, borrToken, repayAmt, _param.data);
vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: When tokenOut is collToken (user wants to receive collateral token), CloseExactOut is used. The swapInfo.tokenOut is set to borrToken, which is the token whose balance is checked with strict equality in coreCallback.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls reducePos with tokenOut = collToken
2. _reducePosInternal sets swapType = CloseExactOut, swapInfo.tokenOut = borrToken, swapInfo.amtOut = repayAmt
3. Multicall executes: decollateralize → burnTo → callback → repay
4. In callback, coreCallback is called by InitCore
5. coreCallback transfers all collToken to swapHelper
6. SwapHelper calls swapTokensForExactTokens, sends exactly amtOut of borrToken to hook
7. SwapHelper refunds unused collToken to hook
9. If attacker pre-sent 1 wei of borrToken, balance = amtOut + 1, check fails, tx reverts
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The attack vector is straightforward: (1) Attacker monitors mempool for reducePos transactions with tokenOut = collToken. (2) Attacker front-runs by calling borrToken.transfer(hookAddress, 1). (3) When the user's tx executes, coreCallback's strict equality check fails because balanceOf returns amtOut + 1 instead of amtOut. The MoeSwapHelper.swap() for CloseExactOut calls IMoeRouter.swapTokensForExactTokens which outputs exactly amtOut to msg.sender (the hook). The pre-existing 1 wei dust causes the total balance to exceed the expected amount.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided. The vulnerability is inherent in the code logic regardless of deployment configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack is fully validated: (1) Attacker identifies any pending reducePos tx where tokenOut = collToken (CloseExactOut path). (2) Attacker sends 1 wei of borrToken to the MarginTradingHook proxy contract address. Cost: ~1 wei + gas. (3) User's reducePos tx enters coreCallback, SwapHelper returns exactly repayAmt of borrToken, but hook already has 1 extra wei, so balanceOf = repayAmt + 1. (4) The _require(balance == amtOut) check fails, entire multicall reverts. (5) The attack can be sustained indefinitely since the 1 wei remains in the contract after the user's tx reverts. Each new reducePos attempt would still fail because the dust is persistent. Even if the user somehow consumes the dust, the attacker can send another 1 wei.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity DoS. Users cannot close margin positions via CloseExactOut during adverse market conditions. This could force users into liquidation if they cannot reduce their positions. The attack cost is negligible (1 wei of borrToken per persistent DoS). Users can only work around this by using CloseExactIn (tokenOut = borrToken) instead, which changes the trade semantics and may not be desirable. The attacker could profit indirectly by being the liquidator of stuck positions.
recommended_mitigations: Change the strict equality check to a >= check: _require(IERC20(swapInfo.tokenOut).balanceOf(address(this)) >= swapInfo.amtOut, Errors.SLIPPAGE_CONTROL)

Alternatively, snapshot the borrToken balance before the swap and check the delta: uint borrBalBefore = IERC20(swapInfo.tokenOut).balanceOf(address(this)); ... uint borrBalAfter = IERC20(swapInfo.tokenOut).balanceOf(address(this)); _require(borrBalAfter - borrBalBefore == swapInfo.amtOut, Errors.SLIPPAGE_CONTROL);
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy MarginTradingHook, InitCore, LendingPool (for collToken and borrToken), MoeSwapHelper, PosManager with appropriate config. 2. Create a margin position via openPos. 3. Send 1 wei of borrToken directly to the MarginTradingHook contract address. 4. Call reducePos with tokenOut = collToken (triggering CloseExactOut). 5. Verify the transaction reverts with SLIPPAGE_CONTROL error. 6. For comparison, verify that the same reducePos call succeeds without the dust deposit.
uncertainties: The exact line numbers in the proof_of_signal may not match exactly due to formatting differences, but the code pattern is confirmed in the coreCallback function.
While CloseExactIn path is unaffected (no strict equality check), users may not always be able to use it as an alternative depending on their desired trade outcome.
additional_testing_needed: Verify that the dust borrToken persists across failed transactions (it should, since the revert rolls back the multicall but the direct transfer was in a separate transaction)
Test whether the dust can be consumed by a normal CloseExactIn reducePos to clear the hook's borrToken balance
Measure the gas cost of the griefing attack on Mantle to confirm economic feasibility
